target.title: docs: UX-013 ŌĆö design system documentation
target.body:
Adds `ui/docs/design-system/` with 5 files documenting the glass surface system, color tokens with WCAG contrast ratios, motion standards, and an accessibility checklist.

## Files added

- `README.md` ŌĆö index + one-paragraph summary of the design language and five guiding principles
- `glass-surfaces.md` ŌĆö two glass tiers with exact CSS values as-found in source, no-solid-panels rule, `@supports` fallback pattern, anti-patterns
- `color-tokens.md` ŌĆö full token table with dark/light values, intended use, contrast ratios on dark bg, anti-patterns
- `motion.md` ŌĆö duration scale (`--duration-fast/normal/slow`), easing tokens, `prefers-reduced-motion` pattern, animation inventory sourced from actual codebase
- `accessibility.md` ŌĆö WCAG 2.1 AA checklist: contrast minimums, `:focus-visible` pattern, 44px tap targets, skip link, focus trap, ARIA labels, `role="tab"` pattern, reduced-motion, testing tools

All values reflect reality (read directly from `base.css`, `chat/layout.css`, `components.css`), not assumptions.

Prevents design regressions and onboards contributors to the visual language.

Part of UI/UX Enhancement Sprint 2.

target.title: fix(cron): persist resolved delivery target into isolated cron session deliveryContext
target.body:
## Summary

Isolated cron completion delivery can fail with `Channel is required (no configured channels detected)` even when the job explicitly sets `delivery.channel`, because the resolved delivery target is never written back to the isolated cron session entry's `deliveryContext`. Completion-announce paths such as `sessions_send`/`subagent_announce` later resolve the target from the session entry and see an empty context.

This change persists the resolved cron delivery target onto the isolated cron session entry right after `resolveCronDeliveryContext`, so explicit `delivery.channel` survives to the final completion announcer.

Fixes #92460.

## Changes

- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-session-state.ts`: add `setCronSessionDeliveryContextFromResolvedDelivery`, a focused helper that normalizes and writes a resolved delivery target into the session entry's `deliveryContext` only when resolution succeeds and a routable target exists.
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`: call the helper in `prepareCronRunContext` after `resolveCronDeliveryContext` returns.
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-session-state.test.ts`: add regression tests covering explicit target persistence, missing optional fields, failed-resolution no-op, and empty-target no-op.
- `scripts/repro/issue-92460-cron-completion-delivery-context.mts`: standalone reproduction script that exercises the production helper and verifies the explicit channel/to/account/thread survive.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: explicit `delivery.channel` on an isolated cron job is now persisted onto the isolated session entry, so completion-announce target resolution no longer falls back to a channel-less main session.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node v22.19.0, local OpenClaw source checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-92460-cron-completion-delivery-context.mts`
- **Evidence after fix**:
  Terminal output from running the standalone reproduction script against the patched source:
  ```text
  === Reproduction for issue #92460 ===
  Isolated cron session deliveryContext: {
    \"channel\": \"webchat\",
    \"to\": \"controller\",
    \"accountId\": \"default\",
    \"threadId\": \"thread-42\"
  }
  PASS: explicit delivery.channel survives to the session entry deliveryContext
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: the isolated cron session entry now carries a normalized deliveryContext with the explicit channel, to, account, and thread from the resolved delivery target. Before the patch the same script would leave deliveryContext empty and the entry would lose the explicit webchat target.
- **What was not tested**: live channel send integration (no configured webchat gateway in the test environment); only the session-state propagation path and existing cron delivery-target resolution tests were exercised.

## Verification

- [x] `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-92460-cron-completion-delivery-context.mts` passes
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run-session-state.test.ts --run` passes
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts --run` passes
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts --run` passes
- [x] `npx oxlint` on changed files passes
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt` on changed files passes
- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` passes

commands.author: mushuiyu886

target.title: fix(opencode): restore Zen model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix classification: Root-cause provider catalog and routing fix for the bundled `opencode` Zen provider.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High ŌĆö the current PR head has fresh authenticated provider proof, family-specific live adapter proof, and focused regression coverage.
- Restore a plugin-owned OpenCode Zen model catalog for the bundled `opencode` provider.
- Add static rows for the 48 models currently advertised by Zen, plus runtime live discovery for `/zen/v1/models` when credentials are available.
- Restore dynamic model resolution, catalog augmentation, family-specific transport routing, and manifest metadata for manifest-first flows.

Fixes #92479

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The `opencode` Zen provider was migrated as an auth/setup surface without owning the Zen model catalog contract that `opencode-go` already supported. As a result, the provider registry had no source-of-truth model rows, no dynamic resolver for `opencode/<model>` references, and no family-aware transport mapping for Zen model IDs.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The patch restores the missing provider-owned catalog/resolver boundary instead of adding downstream string special cases. Runtime catalog loading now resolves real Zen rows when discovery succeeds, keeps a local seed available when discovery is unavailable, and maps each model family at the provider boundary before the generic LLM adapters run.
- Why it matters / User impact: Users who configure OpenCode Zen can select advertised Zen models directly instead of hand-registering every model or seeing `opencode/<model>` fail as unknown/unavailable.
- What did NOT change: This does not change non-OpenCode providers, does not change `opencode-go` runtime behavior, and does not expand the generic LLM adapter APIs outside this provider's catalog/routing contract.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: The provider plugin is the source-of-truth owner for bundled provider catalog metadata. `check-upstream-fixed` found main does not already cover this PR, and the remaining product decision is seed scope/ownership, not a competing implementation.
- Patch quality notes: The patch-quality warnings are expected for this provider-catalog repair: the static seed is a deliberate offline guard, default/undefined returns are catalog parsing boundaries, and the larger manifest diff is generated catalog metadata backed by live provider proof rather than unrelated refactor.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: The bundled `opencode` Zen provider exposed auth/setup but did not expose a model catalog or dynamic model resolver, so `opencode/<model>` selections failed as unknown or unavailable. This patch restores the provider-owned Zen catalog, keeps a 48-row seed visible when live discovery is unavailable, resolves shared `opencode-go` credentials for the Zen catalog, and routes representative Zen model families through the expected OpenClaw adapters.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2, local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `e5497442`, authenticated OpenCode Zen key present but redacted. This machine requires `NODE_USE_ENV_PROXY=1` plus uppercase proxy variables for Node 22 outbound fetch/SDK traffic.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
source "$EVIDENCE_DIR/context.env"
OPENCODE_API_KEY=<redacted> \
  HTTPS_PROXY="$https_proxy" HTTP_PROXY="$http_proxy" ALL_PROXY="$all_proxy" NO_PROXY="$no_proxy" \
  NODE_USE_ENV_PROXY=1 \
  corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir "$TASK_WORKTREE" exec tsx \
  "$EVIDENCE_DIR/opencode-zen-live-proof.mts" > "$EVIDENCE_DIR/opencode-zen-live-proof.log" 2>&1

corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir "$TASK_WORKTREE" exec vitest run extensions/opencode/index.test.ts
```

- Evidence after fix: Authenticated discovery returned the 48-row Zen catalog with an Authorization header present, the provider catalog resolved using shared `opencode-go` credentials, and representative live completions returned `ok` through the affected OpenClaw adapter families.

```text
OpenCode Zen live proof at 2026-06-13T03:27:09.724Z
worktree=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92495
credential=present (redacted)
node_env_proxy=enabled
authenticated_discovery_models=48
authenticated_discovery_request url=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models authorization_header=present status=200
shared_opencode_go_credential_catalog_models=48
live_request family=Claude model=claude-haiku-4-5 api=anthropic-messages baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen payload_model=claude-haiku-4-5 stream=true max_tokens=64 http_status=200 stopReason=stop text="ok"
live_request family=GPT model=gpt-5-nano api=openai-responses baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 payload_model=gpt-5-nano stream=true max_tokens=64 http_status=200 stopReason=stop text="ok"
live_request family=Gemini model=gemini-3-flash api=google-generative-ai baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 payload_model=gemini-3-flash stream=undefined max_tokens=undefined stopReason=stop text="ok"
live_request family=Qwen model=qwen3.6-plus api=anthropic-messages baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen payload_model=qwen3.6-plus stream=true max_tokens=64 http_status=200 stopReason=stop text="ok"
live_request_attempt family=MiniMax model=minimax-m2.7 api=openai-completions baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 result=failed error="unexpected text: <empty>; content="
live_request family=MiniMax model=minimax-m2.5 api=openai-completions baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 payload_model=minimax-m2.5 stream=true max_tokens=64 http_status=200 stopReason=stop text="ok"
result=passed
```

- Observed result after fix: `opencode` now exposes a 48-model Zen catalog, can populate the catalog from live discovery with a redacted real credential, accepts the shared OpenCode credential path from `opencode-go`, and successfully completes real provider calls through Claude/Anthropic messages, GPT/OpenAI responses, Gemini/Google generative AI, Qwen/Anthropic messages, and MiniMax/OpenAI chat completions routes.
- What was not tested: I did not live-call all 48 Zen models individually. The authenticated discovery proof used the provider-catalog live helper with a direct fetch guard because this local machine's default SSRF-guarded fetch path times out behind the configured proxy; the production no-discovery path remains covered by static catalog tests, and the real model requests above exercised the actual OpenClaw runtime provider adapters. `minimax-m2.7` returned an empty stream body in this account/environment, so the MiniMax family proof used `minimax-m2.5`, which returned `ok` through the same `openai-completions` route.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target test file: `extensions/opencode/index.test.ts` plus live evidence script `opencode-zen-live-proof.mts` stored outside the repo evidence directory.
- Scenario locked in: Regression tests assert the Zen catalog exposes the 48 model rows, dynamic model resolution works for Zen IDs, shared `opencode-go` credentials populate the `opencode` catalog path, and Claude/GPT/Gemini/Qwen/MiniMax families route to the expected adapter/base URL pairs.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: A focused provider test locks the source-of-truth catalog/routing contract without adding broad adapter mocks, while the live proof covers the external provider behavior that unit tests cannot prove.

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92495

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  10 passed (10)
Duration  44.99s
```

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 ŌĆö authenticated discovery and representative live requests:** Addressed by the redacted authenticated `/zen/v1/models` request (`authorization_header=present status=200`) and live `ok` completions through Claude, GPT, Gemini, Qwen, and MiniMax adapter families.
- **RF-002 ŌĆö stronger real behavior proof:** Addressed by the authenticated external Zen proof above; this is no longer limited to public discovery, static catalog listing, or unit tests.
- **RF-003 ŌĆö shared credentials and affected adapters:** Addressed by `shared_opencode_go_credential_catalog_models=48` plus live requests through `anthropic-messages`, `openai-responses`, `google-generative-ai`, and `openai-completions`.
- **RF-004 ŌĆö static catalog ownership/synchronization risk:** The 48-row seed is intentionally provider-owned so the Zen provider remains usable when live discovery is unavailable. Live discovery is still preferred whenever credentials are present; if the provider owner wants a smaller seed or different ownership policy, that is a product/maintenance decision rather than a code defect in this patch.
- **RF-005 ŌĆö routing defect and maintainer decision boundary:** The routing defect is covered by tests and live requests. The remaining maintainer decision is the acceptable seed scope for Zen, not whether the restored catalog/routing behavior works.

## Merge risk

- Risk labels considered: `merge-risk: ­¤Ü© compatibility`, `merge-risk: ­¤Ü© auth-provider`, provider catalog/routing, generated manifest metadata, and large-diff catalog seed review cost.
- Risk explanation: The compatibility risk is that a provider-owned seed can drift from Zen if the upstream catalog changes before live discovery runs. The auth-provider risk is bounded to resolving the same OpenCode credential across `opencode` and `opencode-go`; no key is stored in the repo or PR body, and live proof used a redacted environment variable only. The large diff is mostly generated manifest/catalog rows rather than a cross-cutting runtime refactor.
- Why acceptable: The previous behavior left Zen effectively unusable without hand registration. The patch restores the provider contract at its owner boundary, keeps live discovery as the preferred source when credentials are present, preserves a safe no-credential/no-discovery local seed, and now has real provider proof across the affected adapter families.

commands.author: SYU8384

target.title: Memory/QMD: isolate mcporter sidecars per agent
target.body:
## Summary

Isolates QMD mcporter configuration per agent while preserving user/workspace-owned mcporter definitions that must stay external. For agents without an explicit user-owned mcporter server definition, OpenClaw generates a per-agent stdio config under `$STATE/agents/<agentId>/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json` with manager-scoped QMD env vars.

Supersedes #79745. This replacement PR keeps the QMD/mcporter fix and drops the unrelated Skill Workshop i18n commit that accidentally widened the superseding branch.

## Linked context

Related #79745.

## Behavior addressed

- Remote QMD servers with auth material (`headers`, env, URL credentials, refresh metadata, token-like query params) stay external and use the original mcporter config.
- Servers with `lifecycle`, daemon logging, relative `cwd`, or relative `path` are treated as user-owned and kept external.
- Bare stdio QMD servers are rewritten into a generated per-agent config under OpenClaw state.
- `startDaemon=false` omits the keep-alive lifecycle in the generated config so mcporter does not auto-launch the daemon.
- Snake-case remote endpoint keys (`base_url`, `server_url`) are recognized alongside camelCase.
- Project-scoped mcporter configs are probed before legacy home fallback.
- Discovery mode uses a clean `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` so OpenClaw state dirs do not shadow user/project configs.
- External-mode calls set `MCPORTER_CONFIG` to the discovered source path so the original config is reused.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: QMD memory search through mcporter must keep each agent on its own mcporter config/daemon state while preserving configured user/workspace mcporter servers that contain auth, lifecycle, logging, cwd/path, or other mcporter-owned behavior.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node v24.15.0, mcporter 0.10.2, qmd 2.1.0 (bab86d5), OpenClaw built from `fix/qmd-mcporter-agent-isolation`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
pnpm build
export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_DIR="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/config"
export XDG_CONFIG_HOME="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/xdg-config"
export XDG_CACHE_HOME="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/xdg-cache"
export QMD_CONFIG_DIR="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/qmd-config"
unset MCPORTER_CONFIG

./openclaw.mjs agents add proof-a --non-interactive --workspace "$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-a"
./openclaw.mjs agents add proof-b --non-interactive --workspace "$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-b"
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.backend qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.command qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.enabled true
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.serverName qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.startDaemon false

./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "test" --agent proof-a
./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "test" --agent proof-b
```

- Evidence after fix:

Per-agent generated configs are isolated:

```text
$STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  command: qmd
  args: ["mcp"]
  XDG_CONFIG_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-config
  QMD_CONFIG_DIR: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-config/qmd
  XDG_CACHE_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-cache
  lifecycle: absent when startDaemon=false

$STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  command: qmd
  args: ["mcp"]
  XDG_CONFIG_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-config
  QMD_CONFIG_DIR: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-config/qmd
  XDG_CACHE_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-cache
  lifecycle: absent when startDaemon=false
```

`startDaemon=true` adds the keep-alive lifecycle:

```text
$STATE/agents/proof-daemon/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  lifecycle: { mode: "keep-alive", idleTimeoutMs: 300000 }
```

Remote auth config stays external:

```text
User ~/.mcporter/mcporter.json contains:
  base_url: https://qmd.example.com/mcp
  headers.Authorization: Bearer redacted-token

./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "external" --agent proof-external
  mcporter call qmd.query ... failed with ENOTFOUND qmd.example.com

No $STATE/agents/proof-external/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json was generated; auth material was not copied into OpenClaw state.
```

Project config wins over home config:

```text
$WORKSPACE/config/mcporter.json contains qmd cwd ./project-cwd and lifecycle keep-alive.
./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "project" --agent proof-project
  mcporter retries the configured qmd server and preserves the relative cwd from project config.
```

- Observed result after fix: bare stdio QMD servers are isolated per agent, daemon startup follows `startDaemon`, and user-owned remote/project/lifecycle/logging/cwd/path mcporter definitions remain external instead of being copied into generated OpenClaw-managed state.
- What was not tested: Windows paths. Broken explicit `MCPORTER_CONFIG` still silently falls back to generated mode for the default `qmd` server; this is pre-existing behavior.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the remote-auth proof used a redacted/unresolvable test endpoint to prove OpenClaw reused the external mcporter config without copying auth-bearing state.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): previous reviews on #79745 confirmed current main passed QMD-scoped XDG env into mcporter, which could redirect mcporter discovery/state into per-agent QMD paths.

## Tests and validation

Fresh validation after cleaning this superseding PR branch on head `e1ec585278`:

```sh
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts
```

Observed local result:

```text
qmd-manager.test.ts: 157 tests passed
agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts: 33 tests passed
git diff --check: clean
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, in the safer direction: auth-bearing mcporter definitions stay external and are not copied into OpenClaw-managed generated state.

Highest-risk area: mcporter config discovery and upgrade compatibility for existing QMD memory users.

Mitigation: preserve explicit/project/home mcporter config sources when the server definition contains user-owned behavior, generate per-agent configs only for bare stdio QMD servers, and cover the classifier/generation matrix in `qmd-manager.test.ts`.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper re-review on the cleaned head.

Still waiting on: maintainer/ClawSweeper review after the branch cleanup.

Addressed bot/reviewer comments:

- Supersedes accidentally closed #79745.
- Kept the QMD/mcporter fixes from #79745.
- Dropped unrelated Skill Workshop i18n commit from this superseding PR.
- Rebased onto current `upstream/main`.

target.body:
## Summary
- Add `tools.toolSearch.mode: "directory"` so large catalogs can expose a compact prompt directory plus `tool_describe`/`tool_call` controls while deferring most schemas.
- Hydrate likely or requested directory tools from the catalog, including missing tool calls, while preserving sequential execution contracts.
- Keep client tools directly visible in directory mode and document the new mode.

Fork attribution: based on JuanHuaXu/openclaw `codex/tool-search-directory-mode`, compared from `codex/fix-post-tool-continuation-guard`.

## Verification

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: `tools.toolSearch.mode: "directory"` keeps authorized catalog schemas deferred while preserving normal validation, hooks, execution, result normalization, replay, and provider continuation. Exact head `55d9e5697968c0ec64d487eff32db59e284d32ef` also honors wildcard client-tool allowlists, keeps external tool descriptions out of the system prompt, converts deferred-resolver failures into paired tool errors, keeps client-owned names out of native capability guidance, and tracks the intentional config/plugin-SDK contract changes.

target.title: Bug: doctor falsely warns local memory embeddings are not ready
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw doctor` reports that local embeddings are ŌĆ£not confirmed readyŌĆØ even when `openclaw memory status --deep` confirms the full memory stack is healthy.

## Version

OpenClaw `2026.6.6` (`8c802aa`)

## Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider` as `local` using the bundled Hugging Face GGUF embedding model.
2. Run `openclaw memory status --deep`.
3. Observe `Embeddings: ready`, `Vector store: ready`, `Semantic vectors: ready`, and `FTS: ready`.
4. Run `openclaw doctor` or `openclaw doctor --deep`.
5. Doctor still reports:

```text
Memory search provider is set to "local", but local embeddings are not confirmed ready.
Gateway probe: memory embedding readiness not checked; run `openclaw memory status --deep` to probe
```

`openclaw doctor --lint --json` simultaneously returns `{"ok":true,"checksRun":22,"checksSkipped":0,"findings":[]}`.

## Cause observed in installed build

`doctor-B9vJA5aF.js` defines `SKIPPED_MEMORY_EMBEDDING_PROBE` with `checked: false`. The doctor flow supplies that result to `doctor-memory-search-C3qfCGTd.js`, whose local-provider branch emits the warning unless `gatewayMemoryProbe.checked && gatewayMemoryProbe.ready`.

The `--deep` CLI option currently does not cause this memory probe to be checked, so it produces the same false warning.

## Expected behavior

Doctor should either:

- run the memory embedding readiness probe (especially with `--deep`) and suppress the warning when ready, or
- report that readiness was not tested as neutral/informational rather than implying the configured local provider is broken.

## Actual health

The authoritative deep memory command passes all components, so this appears to be a doctor false positive rather than a memory runtime failure.

commands.author: zenglingbiao

target.title: fix(test): exclude browser cache and profile directories from live auth staging (fixes #91893)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: `test/test-env.ts` `copyDirIfExists` uses unfiltered `fs.cpSync` to copy `LIVE_EXTERNAL_AUTH_DIRS` (which includes `.gemini`) into a temp home during live test setup. On machines where `.gemini` contains browser profile data (e.g. Antigravity browser), this copies multi-GB caches and fills `/tmp` with `ENOSPC`.
- **Root Cause**: `copyDirIfExists` calls `fs.cpSync` with `{ recursive: true, force: true }` but no `filter` option. When `.gemini` contains browser caches (`antigravity-browser-profile`, `browser_recordings`, `Service Worker` directories), the entire multi-GB tree is copied blindly.
- **Fix**: Add a `filter` to `copyDirIfExists` that skips known browser cache and profile directories. Auth files (`.gemini/auth.json`) and other legitimate config are still copied.
- **What changed**:
  - `test/test-env.ts` ŌĆö new `COPY_EXCLUDE_PATTERNS` constant and `filter` option on `fs.cpSync`
  - `test/test-env.test.ts` ŌĆö new test case verifying cache directories are excluded from live auth staging
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `LIVE_EXTERNAL_AUTH_DIRS` list is unchanged ŌĆö `.gemini` is still staged
  - Non-cache `.gemini` content (auth tokens, config) is still copied as before
  - Other callers of `copyDirIfExists` (credentials, external-plugins) are unaffected

### Reproduction

1. Install a browser that populates `.gemini/antigravity-browser-profile/Default/Cache/` with several GB of data
2. Set `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1` and run live tests that call `stageLiveTestState`
3. **Before this PR**: `fs.cpSync` recursively copies the entire `.gemini` tree including multi-GB browser cache ŌåÆ `ENOSPC` on `/tmp`
4. **After this PR**: browser cache and profile directories are filtered out; only auth/config files are staged

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (91893)**: `copyDirIfExists` in live test staging copies multi-GB browser cache and profile directories from `.gemini`, causing ENOSPC on `/tmp`.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 ŌĆö temp-directory fixture exercising the `stageLiveTestState` code path through `installTestEnv`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/test-env.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/0668001395/openclawProject1

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
   Start at  09:57:36
   Duration  1.96s

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 10.24s
```

**Observed result after fix**: The new `excludes browser cache and profile directories from live auth staging` case confirms that `antigravity-browser-profile`, `browser_recordings`, and `Service Worker` directories are fully excluded from the staged temp home, while legitimate auth files (`.gemini/auth.json`) are still correctly copied. The 4 pre-existing installTestEnv cases continue to exercise config/auth staging without regression.

**What was not tested**: Live ENOSPC reproduction was not driven ŌĆö that requires a macOS machine with multi-GB `.gemini` browser cache. The source-code path through `fs.cpSync` with the new filter is verified via the temp-directory test fixture.

**Repro confirmation**: The new test case creates a temp home containing `.gemini` with both auth files and simulated browser cache directories, then verifies the cache directories are absent from the staged output. Before this patch, the same fixture would copy all directories without filtering.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: The exclusion patterns are string-based and could theoretically match a legitimate path name.
  **Mitigation**: Patterns target names that are specific to browser internals (`antigravity-browser-profile`, `Service Worker/CacheStorage`, `GPUCache`, `Cache_Data`). These are extremely unlikely to appear in auth/config paths. The filter only applies to the live test staging path, not production runtime.
- **Risk**: A future browser version could use different cache directory names.
  **Mitigation**: The patterns are easy to extend. The filter is non-destructive ŌĆö at worst, a new cache directory would not be excluded until the pattern list is updated.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] test infrastructure

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/test-env.test.ts`
- The new test case verifies: auth files are copied, cache directories are excluded

### Review Findings Addressed

N/A (initial submission)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #91893

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165ŌĆō180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` ŌĆö that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes ŌĆö for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` ŌĆö the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` ŌĆö export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` ŌĆö replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` ŌĆö add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` ŌĆö `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165ŌĆō180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` ŌåÆ `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 ŌĆö Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` ŌåÆ `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` ŌåÆ `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` ŌåÆ `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` ŌåÆ `true`; null/undefined ŌåÆ `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: fix(health-monitor): add reconnect grace for gateway reconnects
target.body:
Repairs the existing #45712 branch rather than replacing it.

Summary:
- add shared reconnectGraceMs/lastDisconnectAt handling so running-but-recently-disconnected channels are not restarted before provider reconnect grace expires
- consume typed lastDisconnect.at from runtime snapshots without unsafe casts
- update health monitor and readiness call sites plus focused regression coverage for recent disconnects, expired disconnects, stopped channels, stale sockets, active runs, and restart caps

Validation:
- pnpm check:changed

Credit: based on #45712 by @cass-clearly / Cass.

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-157023-autonomous-smoke

target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

## Credit
Fixes the non-security path tracked in #48360. Uses reproduction evidence from #62762 and #73535, with historical evidence from #51357 and #42019. Thanks @RacecarGuy, @justinschille, @DJBlackhawk, @tianyaqpzm, and @0xrsydn.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` ŌåÆ `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner ŌĆö the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function ŌĆö bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function ŌĆö pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check ŌĆö has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key ŌĆö so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` ŌåÆ `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` ŌĆö 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits ŌĆö the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) ŌĆö the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165ŌĆō180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` ŌĆö that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes ŌĆö for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` ŌĆö the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` ŌĆö export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` ŌĆö replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` ŌĆö add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` ŌĆö `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165ŌĆō180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` ŌåÆ `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 ŌĆö Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` ŌåÆ `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` ŌåÆ `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` ŌåÆ `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` ŌåÆ `true`; null/undefined ŌåÆ `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.
